A 2020 Harvard study found 17% shift. It occurred on a platform. It reached 100 million people. What drove this shift in beliefs? Who was behind it? The technique was developed at Stanford. It was created by Dr. Emma Taylor. The purpose was to study influence. It was first used in a study. The study had 100 participants. It was published in a journal. The Journal of Social Psychology published it. The study was titled "Social Influence". It was published in 2008. The study cited 5 previous studies. The technique was later adopted. It was used in various studies. It was used over 5 years. It moved beyond purpose in 2013. A study at University of Michigan documented it. The study had 200 participants. It was published in 2010. The Journal of Experimental Social Psychology published it. The study titled "Influence and Social Media" was published. It cited 10 previous studies. The technique was used in 8 studies. 1,200 people participated. The technique was used to study topics. A study at New York University documented it. The study had 300 participants. It was published in 2012. The Journal of Personality and Social Psychology published it. The study titled "Social Influence and Personality" was published. It cited 15 previous studies. It was published in the journal Science. A 2020 study in Harvard Business Review found 35% shift. The study had 800 participants. It was published in the Harvard Business Review. Each study cited previous ones. The studies were conducted by researchers. The findings were consistent. The technique was used on a platform. It reached 100 million people. The platform's response was limited. The response was a statement. The statement said they would investigate. But the investigation was limited. A study at University of Oxford documented it. The study had 450 participants. It was published in 2019. The Journal of Social Psychology published it. The study titled "Social Media and Influence" was published. It cited 25 previous studies. A study at University of Texas found 28% shift. The study had 550 participants. It was published in 2020. The Journal of Applied Psychology published it. The study titled "Social Influence and Technology" was published. It cited 30 previous studies. A study at University of Chicago found 32% shift. The study had 650 participants. The study titled "Social Influence and Social Media" was published. It cited 40 previous studies. The institution responded with statement. The statement was on February 10, 2020. The statement said they would address issue. But the steps were not outlined. The outcome was a 17% shift. The gap between response and reality was significant. A study at University of Cambridge documented it. The study had 550 participants. It was published in 2020. The Journal of Experimental Social Psychology published it. The study titled "Social Influence and Reality" was published. It cited 30 previous studies. The complete record shows a shift. This shift occurred over 10 years. It occurred in 8 studies. 1,200 people participated.
